column2: When predicting real estate prices, relevant features such as square meters, number of bedrooms, and bathrooms are important. However, the owner's name is not relevant to the price and should not be used, as it has no causal relationship to property value.

Supervised learning can solve two main types of problems: Regression (predicting continuous values such as prices or temperatures) and Classification (predicting categories such as spam/not spam). The difference lies in the type of output: numerical vs. categorical.

Supervised learning requires labeled data with inputs (feature values) and their corresponding outputs (labels). These labels act as "supervisors" and guide the algorithm in learning the mapping from inputs to outputs.

The training phase in supervised learning is the phase in which the algorithm observes the labeled data and learns a model that maps an input to an output. The model adjusts its parameters to learn the relationship between inputs and outputs.

Labels are the correct outputs provided in the labeled data. They represent the "answers" that the model should learn to predict. In classification problems, labels are categories; in regression problems, they are numerical values.

Labels act as supervisors during the learning process and guide the algorithm in creating a model that can independently generate labels for new, unseen data. They are essential for supervised learning.

Training data are used to train the model and learn the relationship between inputs and outputs. Test data are used to evaluate the performance of the learned model on unseen data and test its generalization ability.

Supervised learning is suitable for tasks with labeled data such as spam detection, real estate price prediction, or image classification. Clustering, on the other hand, is an unsupervised learning task, as no labels are available.

The typical workflow for developing a supervised learning model includes: question formulation, data collection, data preparation, model development, model evaluation, and model deployment. This iterative process ensures that the model is robust and ready for use.
